section: Microstructures, mechanical properties and deformation of near-rapidly solidified low-density Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr steels

context_update.summary: This chunk introduces the study of Cr addition (0, 3, 6 wt.%) on microstructures and properties of near-rapidly solidified Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C steel strips. The strips were fabricated by centrifugal near-rapid solidification with a cooling rate of ~1–1×10^4 K/s. Table 1 lists the measured chemical compositions. Cr addition increased δ-ferrite volume fraction and size while decreasing κ-carbide precipitation. As-cast yield strength decreased with Cr but UTS and elongation did not change obviously. After 20% cold rolling, Cr-containing strips showed higher YS and UTS than Cr-free strip, attributed to the formation of high-density slip bands facilitated by reduced κ-carbides. The cold-rolled 3Cr strip achieved UTS of 1270 MPa and total elongation of 21%. A double hardening mechanism (Slip-band and Microband) was proposed, with nanoscale κ-carbides controlling early-stage strain hardening rate. The chunk sets the stage for microstructural and deformation analysis.
processing_route: Near-rapid solidification: centrifugal casting, copper mold, 600 rpm, cooling rate ~1–1×10^4 K/s
microstructure: δ-ferrite (volume fraction, size)
κ-carbide precipitation
slip bands
properties: yield strength
ultimate tensile strength
total elongation
UTS=1270 MPa, El=21% for cold-rolled 3Cr strip
mechanisms: Slip-band and Microband double hardening
κ-carbides influence early-stage strain hardening rate

Sample aliases: 0Cr steel; 3Cr steel; 6Cr steel

Properties: yield strength; ultimate tensile strength; total elongation; UTS=1270 MPa, El=21% for cold-rolled 3Cr strip
Mechanisms: Slip-band and Microband double hardening; κ-carbides influence early-stage strain hardening rate

context_update.summary: This chunk completes the introduction and provides the full experimental section. It details the near-rapid solidification fabrication route (centrifugal casting in argon, copper mold at 600 rpm, cooling rate ~1–1×10^4 K/s, as-cast strip size 80×60×2.5 mm). Subsequent heat treatment routes are defined: as-cast annealed (600 °C/3 h, 'annealed'), stress relief (400 °C/1 h) + 20% cold rolling ('CR20%'), and additional 600 °C/3 h annealing ('CR20%+annealed'). Measured densities (6.71, 6.69, 6.66 g/cm³ for 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr) and chemical analysis (ICP, Table 1) are reported. Detailed characterization conditions are given: XRD (Rigaku, λ=0.154056 nm, 30-100°, 4°/min), SEM (etching 3.3% nitric, 3.3% acetic, 0.1% HF, 3 s; imaging 0.5 mm depth; IPP phase fraction), TEM (twin-jet -30°C/30V, 10% perchloric/90% alcohol, JEOL-2010F 200 kV), and tensile testing (room temp, 2.5×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹, 4 mm gauge width, 12 mm gauge length, GB/T-228.1-2010). Tensile data from Table 2 for as-cast and annealed conditions of all three Cr levels are provided, showing YS, UTS, TE, and UTS×TE. The chunk establishes the full experimental matrix and baseline mechanical properties for subsequent microstructure-deformation discussion.
processing_route: Centrifugal near-rapid solidification: argon, copper mold, 600 rpm, cooling rate ~1–1×10^4 K/s
Stress relief: 400 °C / 1 h before cold rolling
Cold rolling: 20% reduction, multipass, twin roll mill Φ160 (CR20%)
properties: Density: 0Cr 6.71, 3Cr 6.69, 6Cr 6.66 g/cm³
mechanisms: 20% cold rolling eliminates solidification shrinkage micropores

context_update.summary: This chunk completes the experimental methods (XRD, SEM, TEM, tensile test details) and presents initial results for the as-cast and annealed steel strips. XRD reveals that strips consist of austenite and retained δ-ferrite; a double-peak (43.5–45°) corresponds to (110)δ and (220)DO3. Cr addition promotes δ→DO3 transformation, and annealing converts nearly all ferrite to DO3. SEM quantifies δ-ferrite volume fractions: as-cast 0Cr 3%, 3Cr 12%, 6Cr 18%; annealed 6%, 14%, 24%. Mechanical tests show Cr decreases YS but not elongation; annealing raises YS by 131, 277, 141 MPa for 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, and the as-cast 6Cr achieves the highest UTS×TE (38.0 GPa%). Strain hardening curves reveal a hump for Cr-free as-cast strip that weakens with Cr, and after annealing a double hump (0Cr), platform (3Cr), or monotonic decrease (6Cr). TEM analysis quantifies κ-carbides (volume fraction, size, lattice parameter) and DO3 phase in δ-ferrite: in 0Cr κ=3 vol%, 6 nm, aκ=0.387 nm, aγ=0.375 nm, DO3=26±3 nm; in 3Cr κ reduced, aκ=0.387 nm, aγ=0.376 nm, DO3=30±3 nm; in 6Cr κ barely visible, aκ=0.389 nm, aγ=0.384 nm, DO3=28±4 nm. Figures 7–9 present XRD, SEM, and stress-strain curves for CR20% and CR20%+annealed conditions, but their findings are not yet discussed. The next chunk should discuss deformation microstructures of cold-rolled strips.
processing_route: near-rapid solidification (centrifugal casting, copper mold 600 rpm, cooling rate ~1–1×10^4 K/s)
key_variables: processing state: as-cast, annealed, CR20%, CR20%+annealed
microstructure: γ-austenite matrix
δ-ferrite (volume fraction 3–24%)
ordered DO3 phase in δ-ferrite (size 26–30 nm)
κ-carbide in γ (volume fraction up to 3%, size 6 nm)
morphology change from short rod to block by Cr
properties: YS decreases with Cr addition
annealing increases YS (by 131, 277, 141 MPa for 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr)
as-cast 6Cr UTS×TE = 38.0 GPa%
strain hardening hump weakens with Cr
annealed 0Cr double hump, 3Cr platform, 6Cr monotonic decrease
mechanisms: Cr promotes δ→DO3 transformation
Cr reduces κ-carbide precipitation
strain hardening behavior influenced by Cr and annealing

context_update.summary: This chunk presents microstructures and mechanical properties of CR20% and CR20%+annealed Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr strips. XRD/SEM show retained γ and δ-ferrite, with δ-ferrite volume fractions of 5% (0Cr), 12% (3Cr), and 18% (6Cr) after cold rolling; 600°C annealing does not significantly alter the microstructure. Tensile tests give UTS of 1154 MPa (0Cr), 1270 MPa (3Cr), and 1264 MPa (6Cr) for CR20%, with Cr increasing both YS and UTS (opposite to as-cast condition); CR20%+annealed strength remains similar. Strain hardening rate decreases with strain; Cr increases the rate, while annealing lowers early-stage hardening for 0Cr and 3Cr. TEM reveals {111} planar slip bands in CR20% γ with spacings of 371±47 nm (0Cr), 100±15 nm (3Cr), and 45±13 nm (6Cr), responsible for strengthening. Annealed strips contain κ-carbides: 7 vol%/8 nm (0Cr) and 4 vol%/4 nm (3Cr); Cr suppresses κ-carbide by expanding Al/C solid solubility. In CR20%+annealed, κ-carbides precipitate at dislocation entanglements but slip bands dominate strengthening. Figures 7–12 are covered. Subsequent chunks should address deformation mechanisms in detail.
processing_route: Cold rolling 20% reduction after stress relief annealing (400°C/1h)
microstructure: γ-austenite with {111} planar slip bands of spacing 45–371 nm
δ-ferrite volume fraction 5–18% (depends on Cr and condition)
DO3 ordered phase in δ-ferrite
κ-carbides in annealed γ: 7 vol%/8 nm (0Cr), 4 vol%/4 nm (3Cr), negligible in 6Cr
κ-carbides precipitate at dislocation entanglements in CR20%+annealed
properties: CR20% UTS: 0Cr 1154 MPa, 3Cr 1270 MPa, 6Cr 1264 MPa
Strain hardening rate decreases with strain; Cr increases it, annealing decreases early stage
Yield strength after cold rolling increases with Cr
CR20%+annealed shows no significant change in YS and UTS compared to CR20%
mechanisms: Cr reduces κ-carbide precipitation by expanding Al/C solid solubility
Slip band spacing controls cold-rolling strengthening: smaller spacing gives higher strength
In CR20%+annealed, slip bands become dominant strengthening factor over κ-carbides
Solid solution strengthening from Cr explains higher YS in as-cast 6Cr despite fewer κ-carbides

Summary: Figures 7–9 present XRD, SEM, and stress-strain curves for CR20% and CR20%+annealed conditions, but their findings are not yet discussed. The next chunk should discuss deformation microstructures of cold-rolled strips. This chunk presents microstructures and mechanical properties of CR20% and CR20%+annealed Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr strips. XRD/SEM show retained γ and δ-ferrite, with δ-ferrite volume fractions of 5% (0Cr), 12% (3Cr), and 18% (6Cr) after cold rolling; 600°C annealing does not significantly alter the microstructure. Tensile tests give UTS of 1154 MPa (0Cr), 1270 MPa (3Cr), and 1264 MPa (6Cr) for CR20%, with Cr increasing both YS and UTS (opposite to as-cast condition); CR20%+annealed strength remains similar. Strain hardening rate decreases with strain; Cr increases the rate, while annealing lowers early-stage hardening for 0Cr and 3Cr. TEM reveals {111} planar slip bands in CR20% γ with spacings of 371±47 nm (0Cr), 100±15 nm (3Cr), and 45±13 nm (6Cr), responsible for strengthening. Annealed strips contain κ-carbides: 7 vol%/8 nm (0Cr) and 4 vol%/4 nm (3Cr); Cr suppresses κ-carbide by expanding Al/C solid solubility. In CR20%+annealed, κ-carbides precipitate at dislocation entanglements but slip bands dominate strengthening. Figures 7–12 are covered. Subsequent chunks should address deformation mechanisms in detail.

TEM reveals {111} planar slip bands in CR20% γ with spacings of 371±47 nm (0Cr), 100±15 nm (3Cr), and 45±13 nm (6Cr), responsible for strengthening. Annealed strips contain κ-carbides: 7 vol%/8 nm (0Cr) and 4 vol%/4 nm (3Cr); Cr suppresses κ-carbide by expanding Al/C solid solubility. In CR20%+annealed, κ-carbides precipitate at dislocation entanglements but slip bands dominate strengthening. Figures 7–12 are covered. Subsequent chunks should address deformation mechanisms in detail.
Material systems: Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (x=0, 3, 6 wt.%) low-density steel strips; Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (x=0, 3, 6 wt%) low-density steel strips; Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (x=0, 3, 6 wt.%) steel strips
Sample aliases: 0Cr steel; 3Cr steel; 6Cr steel; 0Cr; 3Cr; 6Cr; annealed steel strips; CR20%
Processing route: Near-rapid solidification: centrifugal casting, copper mold, 600 rpm, cooling rate ~1–1×10^4 K/s; Centrifugal near-rapid solidification: argon, copper mold, 600 rpm, cooling rate ~1–1×10^4 K/s; As-cast strip dimensions: 80 × 60 × 2.5 mm; Annealing: 600 °C / 3 h (annealed strips); Stress relief: 400 °C / 1 h before cold rolling; Cold rolling: 20% reduction, multipass, twin roll mill Φ160 (CR20%); Post-rolling annealing: CR20% + 600 °C / 3 h (CR20%+annealed); near-rapid solidification (centrifugal casting, copper mold 600 rpm, cooling rate ~1–1×10^4 K/s)
Key variables: Cr content (0, 3, 6 wt.%); cooling rate; cold rolling reduction (20%); Cr content: 0, 3, 6 wt.%; Cooling rate: ~1–1×10^4 K/s; Strip thickness: 2.5 mm; Annealing temperature/time: 600 °C/3 h; Stress relief: 400 °C/1 h
Microstructure: δ-ferrite (volume fraction, size); κ-carbide precipitation; slip bands; γ-austenite matrix; δ-ferrite (volume fraction 3–24%); ordered DO3 phase in δ-ferrite (size 26–30 nm); κ-carbide in γ (volume fraction up to 3%, size 6 nm); morphology change from short rod to block by Cr

context_update.summary: This chunk continues the analysis of cold‑rolled (CR20%) and annealed (CR20%+annealed) Fe‑20Mn‑9Al‑1.2C‑xCr steel strips. TEM on CR20%+annealed samples (Fig. 12) shows that κ‑carbide precipitates at dislocation entanglements in 0Cr and 3Cr strips, but is too small to be resolved in 6Cr. The high density of deformation‑induced slip bands remains the dominant strengthening factor, explaining why yield strength does not increase after annealing. Section 4.2 investigates the effect of Cr on strain hardening. At a true strain of 0.075 (early stage, Fig. 13), 6Cr exhibits finer slip band spacing than 0Cr, leading to higher strain hardening rate; dislocation networks (0Cr) and entanglements (6Cr) are observed. Cr reduces κ‑carbide precipitation, which facilitates slip band formation; κ‑carbides act as obstacles that cause planar slip and glide plane softening. At final strain (tensile fracture, Fig. 14), the microstructures of 0Cr and 6Cr become similar, indicating the weakened role of κ‑carbides. In addition to dense slip bands, well‑developed microbands appear and provide a second hardening mechanism. Dislocation entanglements at ferrite/austenite phase boundaries and a 2.56° low‑angle grain boundary with slip steps are documented. The chunk provides the mechanistic basis for a double hardening mechanism (slip bands + microbands).
microstructure: κ-carbide precipitation at dislocation entanglements (0Cr, 3Cr CR20%+annealed)
κ-carbide too small to observe in 6Cr CR20%+annealed
Slip bands (planar slip characteristic)
Dislocation networks (0Cr early strain)
Dislocation entanglements (6Cr early strain; phase boundary)
Microbands (final strain stage)
Low-angle grain boundary (~2.56°)
properties: Yield strength of CR20%+annealed not significantly increased
Higher strain hardening rate in 6Cr at early strain
Small difference in strain hardening rate between 0Cr and 6Cr at final strain
mechanisms: High density slip bands as main strengthening factor in cold-rolled strips
κ-carbides as energy barrier causing planar slip
Reduction of κ-carbides by Cr promotes slip band formation
Glide plane softening by distorted κ-carbides
κ-carbide effect concentrated at early strain stage
Continuous shearing of κ-carbides at final strain decreases their pinning effect
Microband-induced hardening as second mechanism at final strain
Inconsistent deformation of ferrite and austenite causes phase-boundary dislocation entanglements
unresolved_terms: Reason for the rare slip-band grain-boundary phenomenon at high strain is unclear

context_update.summary: This chunk presents TEM observations (Fig. 14) of annealed 0Cr and 6Cr strips after tensile fracture, showing slip bands, ferrite–microband intersections, shearable grain boundaries, and microbands. The hardening mechanism section (3) discusses that both slip bands and microbands coexist during deformation; slip bands dominate early strain, microbands at final strain. A double hardening mechanism is proposed, explaining the double‑hump strain hardening rate in annealed 0Cr strip (Fig. 3d). The first hump arises from slip‑band refinement, the second from microband formation. Nano‑scale κ‑carbides play a crucial role: when abundant (0Cr), the early hardening rate is low, producing a clear double hump; with fewer κ‑carbides (Cr‑containing), the early rate is higher and the hump weakens. The Conclusions section lists five main findings: (1) ferrite volume fraction and size increase with Cr; (2) κ‑carbide volume fraction decreases with Cr; (3) Cr reduces yield strength of as‑cast/annealed but after 20% cold rolling Cr‑containing strips are stronger (1270 MPa for 3Cr, 1264 MPa for 6Cr, ~21% elongation); (4) Cr‑induced κ‑carbide reduction promotes slip bands and raises early strain hardening; (5) double hardening mechanism confirmed. This chunk completes the paper with no further experimental sections.
microstructure: austenite + ferrite duplex
ferrite irregular shape, volume fraction and size increase with Cr
κ-carbide in austenite, volume fraction decreases with Cr
DO3 ordered phase in ferrite
slip bands and microbands after deformation
properties: yield strength decrease with Cr in as-cast/annealed
UTS of CR20% 3Cr: 1270 MPa, 6Cr: 1264 MPa, elongation ~21%
strain hardening rate double hump in 0Cr, higher early hardening in Cr-containing strips
mechanisms: double hardening: slip-band refinement (early) and microband formation (final)
κ-carbide controls early strain hardening rate
